card.title: The Inverted Yield Curve: Recession Warning
card.teaser: When short-term bonds pay more than long-term ones, something is very wrong. A recession has followed every time — almost.
card.coverImagePrompt: A clean financial chart on a dark terminal-style background showing a yield curve bending downward, glowing blue lines, moody and technical

lessons.title: The signal that makes economists nervous
lessons.body: Every time the 10-year Treasury yield has dropped below the 2-year yield for a sustained stretch since the 1960s, a U.S. recession has followed within about 6 to 24 months. Every single time. Except possibly once. That's why when the curve inverts, you hear market analysts suddenly getting very quiet on live TV.

visual.imagePrompt: A jagged line chart of treasury yields on a dark Bloomberg-style terminal screen

lessons.title: What the 'yield curve' even is
lessons.body: Government bonds come in many time-lengths: 3 months, 2 years, 10 years, 30 years. Plot the interest rate each one pays on a line and you get the yield curve. Normally it slopes up — longer-term bonds pay more because you're locking your money up for longer and taking on more uncertainty.

keyTerms.definition: The annual return you earn on a bond, expressed as a percentage
Debt issued by the U.S. government, considered extremely safe
A chart plotting bond yields across different maturities

visual.imagePrompt: A clean diagram showing a normal upward-sloping yield curve labeled 3M, 2Y, 10Y, 30Y

lessons.title: Why an inversion is weird
lessons.body: An inversion happens when short-term yields rise above long-term yields — meaning the market thinks short-term rates will drop later. Why would rates drop? Usually because the Federal Reserve will be forced to cut them to rescue a slowing economy. So an inverted curve is the bond market quietly saying: we expect a downturn, and rate cuts, within a few years.

visual.imagePrompt: A yield curve dipping downward, with 2Y higher than 10Y, red warning glow

lessons.title: The track record
lessons.body: Research by the San Francisco Fed shows the 10-year minus 3-month spread has inverted before every U.S. recession since 1955, with only one false signal in the mid-1960s. The average lead time is around 12 months. It doesn't say how bad the recession will be — only that the market thinks one is coming.
lessons.funFact: Economist Campbell Harvey put the indicator on the map in his 1986 PhD thesis — before that, it was just a weird statistical oddity.

visual.imagePrompt: A timeline showing U.S. recessions as red bars with yield-curve inversions marked just before each

lessons.title: A weather forecast, not a verdict
lessons.body: The inverted curve is a signal, not a cause. It reflects thousands of traders betting real money on the future of the economy. Predictions can still be wrong — especially when central banks intervene in unusual ways — but when a warning that old and that accurate starts flashing, smart investors pay attention rather than argue with it.

quiz.question: What does an inverted yield curve mean?
options: Long-term yields are higher than short-term yields
Short-term yields are higher than long-term yields
Government bonds have been defaulted on
quiz.explanation: Inversion means short-term bonds are paying more interest than long-term ones — the opposite of normal.

quiz.question: Why does an inverted curve hint at a recession?
options: It shows investors lost faith in the currency
It implies the market expects future rate cuts, usually to fight a slowdown
It forces banks to stop lending
It's required by law during recessions
quiz.explanation: Inversions usually mean the market expects the Fed to cut rates — which typically happens during downturns.

quiz.question: How reliable has the signal been historically?
options: It has predicted every U.S. recession since 1955 with almost no false alarms
It predicts about half of recessions
Only works in Europe, not the U.S.
quiz.explanation: The 10-year minus 3-month version has preceded nearly every U.S. recession since 1955, with one known false signal.
